Leo Ye
@LeoYe_AI
OpenClaw Install Guide（Step-by-step）
Everyone's talking about OpenClaw. The question is: how do you actually set it up?
Two paths. Cloud if you want to be running in ten minutes. Self-host if you want full control. This guide covers both — starting with the fastest.

Two Paths: Cloud or Self-Host

Before we get into specifics, you need to make one decision: do you want to get started immediately with managed hosting, or run OpenClaw yourself on your own infrastructure?
MyClaw.ai
(Cloud) ⭐ vs Self-Host
For most people, the cloud path is the right starting point — you can always migrate to self-hosting later. We'll cover the cloud option first, then walk through every self-hosting method in detail.

📕Path A:
MyClaw.ai
— The Cloud Path (Recommended)

MyClaw.ai
is managed OpenClaw hosting. Someone else handles the server, the updates, the uptime monitoring, and the security patches. You get a running OpenClaw instance connected to your messaging channels without touching a terminal.
This is the fastest way to get a fully operational AI assistant — and for most people, it's the right choice.
How It Works
  1. Sign up at
    myclaw.ai
  2. Pick a model — choose from any officially supported model (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.) with one click. No API key needed. Or bring your own API key if you prefer.
  3. Connect messaging — Telegram, Discord, WhatsApp, etc.
  4. Configure your agent — personality, tools, permissions
  5. Done — your agent is live, 24/7
Total setup time: about 10 minutes.
Why People Choose
MyClaw.ai
  • Zero DevOps — no servers to manage, no SSH, no systemd
  • No API key hassle — pick any model (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.) out of the box. No need to sign up with AI providers separately. Bring your own key if you want to, but you don't have to
  • 24/7 uptime — professionally managed infrastructure with monitoring
  • Automatic updates — you always get the latest OpenClaw features without lifting a finger
  • Support — if something breaks, it's their problem to fix
  • Backups — your configuration and memory are backed up automatically
  • Instant setup — go from zero to a working AI assistant in minutes, not hours
Who It's For
  • Non-technical users who want an AI assistant without the infrastructure headaches
  • Founders and professionals who value their time over the cost difference
  • Teams that need shared access and management features
  • Anyone who wants to start using OpenClaw today without a learning curve
  • People who tried self-hosting and decided they'd rather not maintain servers
MyClaw.ai
isn't a crutch — it's a legitimate choice. The same way most people use Gmail instead of running their own mail server, most people will (and should) use managed hosting for their AI agent. The open-source self-host option exists for those who want it. Both are valid.

Path B: Self-Host OpenClaw

Self-hosting means OpenClaw runs on hardware you control — your Mac, a cloud VPS, a Docker container, even a Raspberry Pi. You own the entire stack.
Here's what you need regardless of platform:
  • Node.js 22+ (the install script handles this for you)
  • An AI provider API key — Anthropic (Claude) is recommended, but OpenAI and others work too
  • A messaging channel — Telegram is the easiest to connect; Discord, WhatsApp, and others are supported
  • ~2 GB RAM minimum (more is better for heavy workloads)

Option 1: macOS — The Easiest Start

If you have a Mac, this is the fastest way to get OpenClaw running on your own hardware. You have two sub-options: the native macOS app (DMG) or the install script.

Sub-option A: macOS App (DMG)

  1. Go to
    openclaw.ai
    (not
    openclaw.com
    !)
  2. Download the macOS DMG
  3. Drag OpenClaw to your Applications folder
  4. Launch it — the onboarding wizard walks you through everything
That's it. The app bundles everything you need.

Sub-option B: Install Script (Terminal)

If you prefer the command line, or want more control, run curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash in your terminal.
This single command does the heavy lifting:
  1. Detects your system — architecture, existing Node.js version, shell type
  2. Installs Node.js 22+ if you don't have it (or upgrades if yours is too old)
  3. Installs OpenClaw globally via npm
  4. Runs the onboarding wizard — walks you through API keys, messaging, and configuration
  5. Installs the daemon so OpenClaw can run in the background
After the script finishes, you'll be dropped into the onboarding wizard. It looks something like this:
💡 🐾 Welcome to OpenClaw! Let's get you set up... It will ask you to select your AI provider (e.g. Anthropic Claude), enter your API key, connect a messaging channel (e.g. Telegram), and enter your bot token.
Follow the prompts. Once the wizard finishes and your messaging channel is connected, you'll have a working AI agent. Expect the whole process — including signing up for an API key and creating a Telegram bot — to take about 30–60 minutes your first time.

Verify It's Working

Once setup completes, run openclaw doctor to check your entire installation — Node version, config files, API key validity, daemon status — and report any issues. Think of it as a health check.
You can also run openclaw status to see the current state of your OpenClaw instance: is the gateway running, which channels are connected, memory usage, etc.

Mac-Specific Considerations

Pros:
  • Fastest path from zero to running on your own hardware
  • Great for trying OpenClaw before committing to a VPS
  • Native macOS app is genuinely polished
Cons:
  • Only runs when your Mac is on and awake
  • macOS aggressive sleep/power management can interrupt the agent
  • If you close the lid, OpenClaw goes dark until you reopen it
Tip: If you're using the CLI version, you can prevent sleep interruptions with caffeinate -s while OpenClaw is running, but this drains your battery faster. For always-on operation, a VPS or
MyClaw.ai
is the better long-term answer.

Option 2: Linux VPS — Best for 24/7 Operation

This is the recommended self-hosting path for anyone who wants OpenClaw running reliably around the clock. A cheap Linux VPS gives you:
  • 24/7 uptime (no sleep issues)
  • Stable network connection
  • Full control over the environment
  • Low cost (~$4–6/month)

Choosing a VPS Provider

You don't need anything fancy. Here are solid options:
Provider Cheapest Viable Plan Notes
  • Hetzner ~€4/mo (CX22) Best value in Europe. ARM options available.
  • DigitalOcean $6/mo (Basic Droplet) Good docs, straightforward UI.
  • Linode (Akamai) $5/mo (Nanode) Reliable, simple pricing.
  • Vultr $6/mo (Cloud Compute) Global regions, hourly billing.
Minimum specs: 2 GB RAM, 1 vCPU, 20 GB disk. Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04 LTS recommended.

Step-by-Step Installation

Step 1: Spin up your VPS
Create an Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04 instance from your chosen provider. Enable SSH access. Most providers give you a root password or let you add an SSH key during creation — SSH key is strongly preferred.
Step 2: SSH into your server
Connect by running ssh root@your-server-ip in your terminal.
Step 3: (Recommended) Create a non-root user
Running OpenClaw as root works, but it's better practice to use a dedicated user:
  • adduser openclaw — create the user
  • usermod -aG sudo openclaw — grant sudo privileges
  • su - openclaw — switch to the new user
Step 4: Run the install script
Run curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash in your terminal.
The script handles everything:
  • Installs Node.js 22+ if it's not present
  • Installs OpenClaw globally
  • Launches the onboarding wizard
  • Sets up systemd to keep OpenClaw running on boot
You'll see output indicating it's detecting your system (Linux x86_64), installing Node.js v22 if not found, installing OpenClaw, and launching the onboarding wizard.
Step 5: Complete the onboarding wizard
Same wizard as the Mac version. You'll configure:
  • AI provider — Anthropic Claude is recommended for the best experience
  • API key — get yours from
    console.anthropic.com
  • Messaging channel — Telegram is the easiest. Create a bot via
    @BotFather
    , copy the token, paste it in
  • Additional settings — timezone, name, personality basics
Step 6: Verify everything is running
  • openclaw doctor — full health check
  • openclaw status — current state
  • openclaw health — quick liveness check
Step 7: Managing the daemon
The install script sets up systemd automatically. Here are the useful commands:
  • openclaw gateway start — start the OpenClaw gateway
  • openclaw gateway stop — stop it
  • openclaw gateway restart — restart (useful after config changes)
  • openclaw gateway status — check if it's running
If you prefer manual process management (or systemd wasn't configured), you can also use tmux or pm2:
  • tmux: Run tmux new -s openclaw, then openclaw gateway start inside the session. Press Ctrl+B then D to detach — OpenClaw keeps running after you disconnect SSH.
  • pm2: Run pm2 start "openclaw gateway start" --name openclaw, then pm2 save and pm2 startup to persist across reboots.

Common Issues & Fixes

"Node.js version too old"
The install script usually handles this, but if you installed Node separately, remove the old version with sudo apt remove nodejs and then re-run curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash to let the script handle it.
"Permission denied" errors
If you're running as a non-root user and see permission errors, run sudo chown -R $(whoami) ~/.openclaw to fix ownership.
"Port already in use"
Another service is using the port OpenClaw needs. Run openclaw doctor to identify the conflict, then either stop the other service or configure a different port in ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json.
"Can't connect to Telegram"
  • Double-check your bot token (get a fresh one from
    @BotFather
    if unsure)
  • Make sure your VPS can reach the internet: run curl -I <
    https://api.telegram.org
    >
  • Check firewall rules: run sudo ufw status
"Gateway won't start after reboot"
Run openclaw gateway status to check what's happening, then openclaw doctor to diagnose, and openclaw gateway restart to fix transient issues.

Option 3: Docker — For Containerization Fans

If you live in Docker-land, OpenClaw has you covered. This approach gives you isolation, reproducibility, and easy cleanup.

Quick Start

Run the following commands in order:
  • git clone <
    https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
    > — clone the repo
  • cd openclaw — enter the directory
  • ./docker-setup.sh — run the setup script
The
docker-setup.sh
script:
  1. Builds the Docker image
  2. Runs the onboarding wizard interactively
  3. Generates a docker-compose.yml with your configuration
  4. Starts OpenClaw via docker compose up -d

Manual Docker Setup

If you want more control, run these commands step by step:
  • git clone <
    https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
    > — clone the repo
  • cd openclaw — enter the directory
  • docker build -t openclaw . — build the Docker image
  • docker run -it -v ~/.openclaw:/root/.openclaw openclaw onboard --install-daemon — run the onboarding wizard with a mounted volume
  • docker compose up -d — start OpenClaw in the background

Docker Tips

  • Persistent config: Mount ~/.openclaw as a volume so your configuration survives container rebuilds
  • Updates: Pull the latest code, rebuild the image, and restart. Run git pull && docker build -t openclaw . && docker compose up -d to do it all at once.
  • Logs: View logs with docker compose logs -f openclaw

Option 4: Raspberry Pi — The Hacker's Choice

Yes, OpenClaw runs on a Raspberry Pi. The install script supports ARM architecture natively.

Requirements

Installation

Same install script — it detects ARM automatically. Run curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash in your terminal.

Pi-Specific Considerations

  • SD card wear: OpenClaw writes logs and memory files. Use a high-endurance SD card (Samsung PRO Endurance, SanDisk MAX Endurance) or boot from an SSD via USB for longevity
  • Cooling: Under sustained load, Pi 4/5 throttles without adequate cooling. A basic heatsink + fan case is cheap insurance
  • Power: Use the official power supply. Underpowered USB chargers cause subtle instability — random crashes, filesystem corruption
  • Memory: On the 4 GB model, avoid running other services alongside OpenClaw. It needs breathing room
  • Network: Wired Ethernet is more reliable than Wi-Fi for an always-on agent
The charm of a Pi setup: your AI assistant runs on a $60 credit-card-sized computer sitting on your desk. There's something satisfying about that.

Option 5: Windows (WSL2)

OpenClaw doesn't run natively on Windows, but it runs perfectly inside WSL2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux).

Step 1: Install WSL2

Open PowerShell as Administrator and run wsl --install. This installs Ubuntu by default. Restart your computer when prompted.

Step 2: Install OpenClaw Inside WSL

Open your Ubuntu terminal (search "Ubuntu" in the Start menu) and run curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash.
From here, it's identical to the Linux VPS instructions above. The onboarding wizard runs, you connect your API keys and messaging, and you're off.

Alternative: Native Windows (PowerShell)

There is also a Windows-native installer if you prefer not to use WSL. Open PowerShell and run iwr -useb <
https://openclaw.ai/install.ps1
> | iex.
This installs OpenClaw directly on Windows. Note that the WSL path is generally more battle-tested and recommended by the community.

Windows Tips

  • WSL2 keeps running when you close the terminal window — your agent stays alive
  • To check, run wsl --list --running
  • For true 24/7 operation on Windows, consider setting WSL to start on boot via Task Scheduler

Other Install Methods

For completeness, here are additional installation paths:

npm/pnpm (Manual)

If you already have Node.js 22+ installed and want to skip the install script:
  • Run npm install -g openclaw@latest to install OpenClaw globally
  • Then run openclaw onboard --install-daemon to complete the setup
Or with pnpm:
  • Run pnpm add -g openclaw@latest to install OpenClaw globally
  • Then run openclaw onboard --install-daemon to complete the setup

From Source

For contributors or anyone who wants to hack on OpenClaw itself:
  • git clone <
    https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
    > — clone the repo
  • cd openclaw — enter the directory
  • pnpm install — install dependencies
  • pnpm build — build the project
  • pnpm link --global — link the CLI globally
  • openclaw onboard --install-daemon — run the setup wizard

Ansible (Production Servers)

For deploying OpenClaw across multiple servers or in a production environment, run curl -fsSL <
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/openclaw-ansible/main/install.sh
> | bash in your terminal.
This pulls the official Ansible playbook that handles the full setup: system dependencies, Node.js, OpenClaw installation, systemd configuration, firewall rules, and log rotation.

Nix

If you're a Nix user, there's a nix-openclaw module available. Check the
docs
for the current flake configuration.

Cloud Platform Guides

Dedicated deployment guides exist for:
  • Hetzner — cheapest reliable option
  • Fly.io
    — easy container deployment
  • GCP — Google Cloud free tier works
  • exe.dev
    — one-click deployment
Find these at
docs.openclaw.ai
.

Self-Host Reality Check

Before you commit to self-hosting, let's be honest about what it involves.
What you're signing up for:
  • Updates — OpenClaw ships updates regularly. You'll want to stay current. This means running the install script again or npm update -g openclaw@latest periodically. Five minutes, but you have to remember to do it.
  • Monitoring — Servers go down. Processes crash. Disks fill up. You need to notice when something breaks. At minimum, check openclaw health daily — or set up a simple uptime monitor.
  • Security — Your server, your responsibility. Keep the OS updated, use SSH keys (not passwords), configure a firewall. Basic hygiene, but it's on you.
  • Debugging — When something doesn't work, you're the first responder. The
    community Discord
    is helpful, but you need to gather logs and describe the problem.
Realistic time commitment: 2–4 hours per month for a smoothly running installation, more in the first few weeks as you iron out kinks. Expect occasional weekends spent debugging when a major update lands or something breaks unexpectedly.
Who shouldn't self-host:
  • If the words "SSH" and "systemd" make you anxious
  • If you don't want to think about infrastructure at all
  • If uptime is critical and you don't have monitoring set up
  • If you value your time and would rather spend hours using your agent than hours setting it up
For those folks,
MyClaw.ai
is the clear answer — go back to Path A and you'll be running in minutes.

Which Path Is Right for You?

Your situation --- Recommendation
"I just want it to work — no servers, no terminals" →
MyClaw.ai
⭐ — the fastest path to a working AI assistant "I want 24/7 but I don't want to manage servers" →
MyClaw.ai
— the whole point is not managing servers
"I'm deploying for a team/company" →
MyClaw.ai
or Ansible depending on your ops capacity
"I want to try OpenClaw this weekend on my Mac" → Mac app — download, install, play
"I want it running 24/7 and I'm comfortable with terminals" → Linux VPS — Hetzner CX22 + install script
"I use Docker for everything" → Docker — clone, build, compose up
"I want a fun hardware project" → Raspberry Pi 5 — nerd cred included
"I'm on Windows" → WSL2 — one extra step, then same as Linux
"I want to contribute to OpenClaw" → From source — clone the repo, hack away
📷

What's Next After Installation

Your agent is running. Now what?

1. Edit Your
SOUL.md

This is the most important file in your OpenClaw setup.
SOUL.md
lives in ~/.openclaw/workspace/ and defines your agent's personality, knowledge, and boundaries. It's a Markdown file — open it in any text editor.
Think of
SOUL.md
as the briefing document you'd give a new executive assistant on their first day. Who are you? What do you care about? What should the agent never do? What tone should it use?
The default
SOUL.md
works fine out of the box, but a personalized one makes the experience dramatically better.

2. Connect Your Channels

If you didn't connect a messaging channel during onboarding, do it now. OpenClaw supports:
  • Telegram — fastest to set up, great for personal use
  • Discord — ideal for communities and teams
  • WhatsApp — works via bridge
  • Web UI — built-in, no setup needed
Telegram is the recommended starting point. Create a bot via
@BotFather
, copy the token, and add it to your OpenClaw config.

3. Give It a Task

Don't overthink the first message. Just say hello:
💡 "Hey, what can you do?"
Then try something real:
💡 "Summarize my last 5 emails" "What's on my calendar tomorrow?" "Remind me to call the dentist at 3pm"
The magic of OpenClaw is that it learns and adapts. The more you use it, the more useful it becomes.

4. Explore the Configuration

Your config lives in ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json. The onboarding wizard sets sensible defaults, but you can customize:
  • AI model preferences
  • Skill modules (tools your agent can use)
  • Memory settings
  • Channel configurations
  • Permissions and safety boundaries
Full documentation:
docs.openclaw.ai

5. Join the Community

  • Discord:
    discord.gg/clawd
    — the most active community hub. Ask questions, share setups, report bugs.
  • GitHub:
    github.com/openclaw/openclaw
    — star the repo, file issues, contribute code.
  • Docs:
    docs.openclaw.ai
    — comprehensive reference for everything.

Quick Reference: Essential Commands

Once OpenClaw is installed, these are the commands you'll use most:

Wrapping Up

OpenClaw is one of those projects where the hardest part isn't the software — it's deciding how you want to run it. Once you make that decision, the actual installation takes minutes.
If you want convenience (recommended for most people): 📕
MyClaw.ai
. Ten minutes, no terminal, no servers, no maintenance. Most people who try both end up here.
If you want control: Self-host on a Linux VPS. Hetzner or DigitalOcean, the install script, and a few hours of setup. You own everything — but you maintain everything too.
If you just want to try it: Download the Mac app or run the install script on whatever machine is in front of you. Just know that the Mac app won't give you 24/7 uptime — for that, you'll want a VPS or
MyClaw.ai
.
The install script for every platform is the same single line: curl -fsSL <
https://openclaw.ai/install.sh
> | bash
That's it. Go build something useful with your new AI assistant.
Have questions? The
OpenClaw Discord
is the fastest place to get help. See you there. 🐾
